title: Prepare approval documents - Additional reviewers | CanadaBuys

breadcrumbs.title: Procurement risk assessment for complexity level 1, 2 and
Approval authorities for Acquisitions Program clients' programs only (other than for a Canadian Commercial Corporation)
Retroactive Treasury Board approval of contracts

structure_blocks.heading: Buyer's Guide Prepare approval documents - Additional reviewers
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Prepare approval documents - Additional reviewers
structure_blocks.text: Additional reviewers As a buyer, you are responsible for ensuring that procurement documents go through the necessary reviews and receive approval by the proper authorities or areas. You must consult various entities before or during the procurement approval process. Reviews may also be done on a discretionary basis, as required. As detailed in the Plan section, some of these additional reviewers include: a quality control officer Legal Services cost and price analysis services Contract Security Program (CSP) a risk management advisor experts on socio-economic considerations the group responsible for contract security a fairness monitor the client You or the approval authority may request a review of the procurement documents by the quality control team of the Specialized Services for Procurement Division. This request should be made on the recommendation of the section Manager, by contacting Contract Quality Control , or Regional Quality Assurance . The appropriate team will provide written feedback. Legal Services must be consulted when the buyer is considering a deviation from standard clauses, departmental standard procurement templates, or commodity or sector-based legally approved templates. Legal Services must review any requests for lessening Canada's full rights at law, a disclaimer, limitation of the contractor's liability, or decrease in the warranty period. The proposed changes must also be acceptable to the client, and form part of the request for approval. A review by Legal Services is within the discretion of the buyer or the approval authority, except in those instances that are mandated by policies and directives within the Acquisitions Program (for example, for sensitive requirements). When a procurement presents any of the conditions noted below and described in the Directive on the Use of Cost and Price Analysis Services (accessible only on the Government of Canada network), it is mandatory that the buyer consult a Price Support Directorate advisor and document the consultation and pricing decisions in the procurement file: Any potential sole-source procurement with a total estimated value of $7,5 million or more, including applicable taxes, options and amendments. Any competitive procurement with a total estimated value of $7,5 million or more, including applicable taxes and options where only one compliant offer is received. Any competitive procurement with a total estimated value of $7,5 million or more, including applicable taxes, with contract provisions for negotiated prices or where prices are likely to be negotiated as a result of a contract amendment. Any competitive procurement, with a total estimated value of $10 million or more, including applicable taxes, options and amendments. Apart from the above mandatory conditions where they must be consulted, Price Support Directorate advisors are always available for consultation on any procurement on a discretionary basis. Refer to the Guideline on the Use of Cost and Price Analysis Services (accessible only on the Government of Canada network) for additional information. You must ensure that any contract security requirement is addressed in the approval documents and that the Contract Security Program (CSP) has been consulted, as appropriate. Clients must: Review the solicitation document, including: Statement of Work Statement of Requirement specifications evaluation criteria Confirm by writing that their input is accurately reflected in both the solicitation document and Treasury Board submission Follow the applicable framework: the generic matrix of responsibilities between PSPC and clients for the procurement of goods and services , or the client-specific agreements with the Department of National Defence A procurement may require modifying or deleting certain conditions. These changes must be discussed with the client before being reflected in the solicitation, contract, standing offer or supply arrangement. This ensures the complete understanding of the extent of the client's rights and responsibilities.

raw_text:
Additional reviewers As a buyer, you are responsible for ensuring that procurement documents go through the necessary reviews and receive approval by the proper authorities or areas. You must consult various entities before or during the procurement approval process. Reviews may also be done on a discretionary basis, as required. As detailed in the Plan section, some of these additional reviewers include: a quality control officer Legal Services cost and price analysis services Contract Security Program (CSP) a risk management advisor experts on socio-economic considerations the group responsible for contract security a fairness monitor the client You or the approval authority may request a review of the procurement documents by the quality control team of the Specialized Services for Procurement Division. This request should be made on the recommendation of the section Manager, by contacting Contract Quality Control , or Regional Quality Assurance . The appropriate team will provide written feedback. Legal Services must be consulted when the buyer is considering a deviation from standard clauses, departmental standard procurement templates, or commodity or sector-based legally approved templates. Legal Services must review any requests for lessening Canada's full rights at law, a disclaimer, limitation of the contractor's liability, or decrease in the warranty period. The proposed changes must also be acceptable to the client, and form part of the request for approval. A review by Legal Services is within the discretion of the buyer or the approval authority, except in those instances that are mandated by policies and directives within the Acquisitions Program (for example, for sensitive requirements). When a procurement presents any of the conditions noted below and described in the Directive on the Use of Cost and Price Analysis Services (accessible only on the Government of Canada network), it is mandatory that the buyer consult a Price Support Directorate advisor and document the consultation and pricing decisions in the procurement file: Any potential sole-source procurement with a total estimated value of $7,5 million or more, including applicable taxes, options and amendments. Any competitive procurement with a total estimated value of $7,5 million or more, including applicable taxes and options where only one compliant offer is received. Any competitive procurement with a total estimated value of $7,5 million or more, including applicable taxes, with contract provisions for negotiated prices or where prices are likely to be negotiated as a result of a contract amendment. Any competitive procurement, with a total estimated value of $10 million or more, including applicable taxes, options and amendments. Apart from the above mandatory conditions where they must be consulted, Price Support Directorate advisors are always available for consultation on any procurement on a discretionary basis. Refer to the Guideline on the Use of Cost and Price Analysis Services (accessible only on the Government of Canada network) for additional information. You must ensure that any contract security requirement is addressed in the approval documents and that the Contract Security Program (CSP) has been consulted, as appropriate. Clients must: Review the solicitation document, including: Statement of Work Statement of Requirement specifications evaluation criteria Confirm by writing that their input is accurately reflected in both the solicitation document and Treasury Board submission Follow the applicable framework: the generic matrix of responsibilities between PSPC and clients for the procurement of goods and services , or the client-specific agreements with the Department of National Defence A procurement may require modifying or deleting certain conditions. These changes must be discussed with the client before being reflected in the solicitation, contract, standing offer or supply arrangement. This ensures the complete understanding of the extent of the client's rights and responsibilities.

Additional reviewers

As a buyer, you are responsible for ensuring that procurement documents go through the necessary reviews and receive approval by the proper authorities or areas. You must consult various entities before or during the procurement approval process. Reviews may also be done on a discretionary basis, as required.

As detailed in the Plan section, some of these additional reviewers include:

a quality control officer

Legal Services

cost and price analysis services

Contract Security Program (CSP)

a risk management advisor

experts on socio-economic considerations

the group responsible for contract security

a fairness monitor

the client

You or the approval authority may request a review of the procurement documents by the quality control team of the Specialized Services for Procurement Division. This request should be made on the recommendation of the section Manager, by contacting Contract Quality Control , or Regional Quality Assurance . The appropriate team will provide written feedback.

Legal Services must be consulted when the buyer is considering a deviation from standard clauses, departmental standard procurement templates, or commodity or sector-based legally approved templates.

Legal Services must review any requests for lessening Canada's full rights at law, a disclaimer, limitation of the contractor's liability, or decrease in the warranty period. The proposed changes must also be acceptable to the client, and form part of the request for approval.

A review by Legal Services is within the discretion of the buyer or the approval authority, except in those instances that are mandated by policies and directives within the Acquisitions Program (for example, for sensitive requirements).

When a procurement presents any of the conditions noted below and described in the Directive on the Use of Cost and Price Analysis Services (accessible only on the Government of Canada network), it is mandatory that the buyer consult a Price Support Directorate advisor and document the consultation and pricing decisions in the procurement file:

Any potential sole-source procurement with a total estimated value of $7,5 million or more, including applicable taxes, options and amendments.

Any competitive procurement with a total estimated value of $7,5 million or more, including applicable taxes and options where only one compliant offer is received.

Any competitive procurement with a total estimated value of $7,5 million or more, including applicable taxes, with contract provisions for negotiated prices or where prices are likely to be negotiated as a result of a contract amendment.

Any competitive procurement, with a total estimated value of $10 million or more, including applicable taxes, options and amendments.

Apart from the above mandatory conditions where they must be consulted, Price Support Directorate advisors are always available for consultation on any procurement on a discretionary basis. Refer to the Guideline on the Use of Cost and Price Analysis Services (accessible only on the Government of Canada network) for additional information.

You must ensure that any contract security requirement is addressed in the approval documents and that the Contract Security Program (CSP) has been consulted, as appropriate.

Clients must:

Review the solicitation document, including: Statement of Work Statement of Requirement specifications evaluation criteria

Statement of Work

Statement of Requirement

specifications

evaluation criteria

Confirm by writing that their input is accurately reflected in both the solicitation document and Treasury Board submission

Follow the applicable framework: the generic matrix of responsibilities between PSPC and clients for the procurement of goods and services , or the client-specific agreements with the Department of National Defence

the generic matrix of responsibilities between PSPC and clients for the procurement of goods and services , or the client-specific agreements with the Department of National Defence

A procurement may require modifying or deleting certain conditions. These changes must be discussed with the client before being reflected in the solicitation, contract, standing offer or supply arrangement. This ensures the complete understanding of the extent of the client's rights and responsibilities.
